phrases.text_local: Excusez-moi, vous parlez anglais ?
phrases.pronunciation: ex-koo-ZAY mwah, voo par-LAY ahn-GLEH
phrases.cultural_note: Always start with 'Excusez-moi'. Many French people speak some English but appreciate you trying French first.

responses.english: Yes, I speak a little English.
responses.phonetic: wee, zhuh PARL uhn PUH ahn-GLEH
responses.local: Oui, je parle un peu anglais.

responses.phonetic: day-zoh-LAY, zhuh nuh PARL pah ahn-GLEH
responses.local: Désolé, je ne parle pas anglais.

responses.english: A little — what do you need?
responses.phonetic: uhn PUH, kess kuh voo voo-LAY
responses.local: Un peu, qu'est-ce que vous voulez ?

phrases.text_local: Je ne comprends pas.
phrases.pronunciation: zhuh nuh kohm-PRAHN pah
phrases.cultural_note: Follow up with 'Pouvez-vous répéter ?' (Could you repeat that?) if you want them to try again more slowly.

responses.phonetic: zhuh veh ray-pay-TAY ploo lahn-tuh-MAHN
responses.local: Je vais répéter plus lentement.

responses.english: Would you like me to write it down?
responses.phonetic: voo voo-LAY kuh zhay-KREEV
responses.local: Vous voulez que j'écrive ?

phrases.text_local: Où sont les toilettes, s'il vous plaît ?
phrases.pronunciation: oo sohn lay twah-LET, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: In France, say 'les toilettes' — never 'la salle de bain' (that's the room with a bathtub). Cafés usually let customers use theirs.

responses.english: It's at the back on the left.
responses.phonetic: seh oh FOHN ah GOHSH
responses.local: C'est au fond à gauche.

responses.english: Over there, go through the door.
responses.phonetic: lah-BAH, pah-SAY lah PORT
responses.local: Là-bas, passez la porte.

responses.english: Sorry, the toilets are for customers only.
responses.phonetic: day-zoh-LAY, lay twah-LET sohn ray-zair-VAY oh klee-AHN
responses.local: Désolé, les toilettes sont réservées aux clients.

phrases.text_local: S'il vous plaît. Merci beaucoup.
phrases.pronunciation: seel voo PLEH. mair-SEE boh-KOO
phrases.cultural_note: These two phrases will get you very far. The French highly value politeness — use them liberally.

responses.phonetic: duh RYEHN
responses.local: De rien !

responses.phonetic: ah-VEK play-ZEER
responses.local: Avec plaisir.

phrases.intent_en: Ask for help in an emergency
phrases.text_local: Aidez-moi, s'il vous plaît ! C'est urgent.
phrases.pronunciation: ay-DAY mwah, seel voo PLEH! seh tur-ZHAHN
phrases.cultural_note: Emergency number in France is 112 (European) or 15 (medical), 17 (police), 18 (fire). All work from any phone.

responses.english: I'm calling for help right away!
responses.phonetic: zhah-PEL lay suh-KOOR too duh SWEET
responses.local: J'appelle les secours tout de suite !

responses.phonetic: kess kee seh pah-SAY
responses.local: Qu'est-ce qui s'est passé ?

phrases.intent_en: Ask someone to speak more slowly
phrases.text_local: Pouvez-vous parler plus lentement, s'il vous plaît ?
phrases.pronunciation: poo-VAY voo par-LAY ploo lahn-tuh-MAHN, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: Pair this with a small apologetic smile. French speakers are generally happy to slow down — they appreciate that you're trying.

responses.phonetic: byehn SOOR! zhuh ruh-koh-MAHNS
responses.local: Bien sûr ! Je recommence.

responses.english: Sorry, I'll try to speak more gently.
responses.phonetic: par-DOHN, zhes-SAY duh par-LAY ploo doos-MAHN
responses.local: Pardon, j'essaie de parler plus doucement.

phrases.intent_en: Ask someone to repeat or write it down
phrases.text_local: Pouvez-vous répéter, s'il vous plaît ? Ou l'écrire ?
phrases.pronunciation: poo-VAY voo ray-pay-TAY, seel voo PLEH? oo lay-KREER
phrases.cultural_note: Asking someone to write it down — especially prices, addresses, or names — is very effective and never considered rude.

responses.phonetic: byehn SOOR, vwah-LAH
responses.local: Bien sûr, voilà.

responses.english: I'll write it down for you.
responses.phonetic: zhuh veh lay-KREER poor voo
responses.local: Je vais l'écrire pour vous.

phrases.intent_en: Explain you speak only a little French
phrases.text_local: Je parle seulement un peu le français.
phrases.pronunciation: zhuh PARL suhl-MAHN uhn PUH luh frahn-SEH
phrases.cultural_note: This sets expectations honestly. Most French people will respond with simpler words, slower speech, or an offer to switch to English.

responses.phonetic: suh neh pah GRAHV — voo voo ahn soor-TAY treh byehn
responses.local: Ce n'est pas grave — vous vous en sortez très bien !

responses.english: We can try in English if you prefer.
responses.phonetic: ohn PUH es-say-AY ahn ahn-GLEH see voo pray-fay-RAY
responses.local: On peut essayer en anglais si vous préférez.

phrases.intent_en: Say you are lost and ask for help
phrases.text_local: Je suis perdu(e). Pouvez-vous m'aider ?
phrases.pronunciation: zhuh swee pehr-DOO. poo-VAY voo may-DAY
phrases.cultural_note: Add 'e' to 'perdu' if you're female (perdue). Showing your destination on your phone while asking is very effective.

responses.english: Where are you trying to go?
responses.phonetic: oo voo-LAY voo zah-LAY
responses.local: Où voulez-vous aller ?

responses.english: It's that way — let me show you.
responses.phonetic: seh par LAH — zhuh voo MOH-ntruh
responses.local: C'est par là — je vous montre.

responses.phonetic: day-zoh-LAY, zhuh nuh swee pah dee-SEE
responses.local: Désolé, je ne suis pas d'ici.

phrases.intent_en: Ask if credit cards are accepted
phrases.text_local: Vous acceptez les cartes bancaires ?
phrases.pronunciation: voo zak-sep-TAY lay kart bahn-KEHR
phrases.cultural_note: France is largely card-friendly, but small markets, village bakeries, and some cafés are cash-only. Always carry a few euros.

responses.phonetic: wee, byehn SOOR
responses.local: Oui, bien sûr.

responses.phonetic: nohn, es-PES suhl-MAHN
responses.local: Non, espèces seulement.

responses.english: Yes, for purchases over ten euros.
responses.phonetic: wee, ah par-TEER duh dee ZUH-roh
responses.local: Oui, à partir de dix euros.

phrases.intent_en: Say you need a doctor urgently
phrases.text_local: J'ai besoin d'un médecin. C'est urgent.
phrases.pronunciation: zhay buh-ZWEHN duhn mayd-SEHN. seh tur-ZHAHN
phrases.cultural_note: France's emergency numbers: 15 (SAMU medical), 17 (police), 18 (fire), 112 (all emergencies, Europe-wide). Pharmacists can also advise on minor issues.

responses.phonetic: zhah-PEL luh KENZ too duh SWEET
responses.local: J'appelle le 15 tout de suite.

responses.english: The hospital is five minutes away.
responses.phonetic: loh-pee-TAL eh tah SEHNK mee-NOOT dee-SEE
responses.local: L'hôpital est à cinq minutes d'ici.

phrases.text_local: Je suis allergique à ___.
phrases.pronunciation: zhuh swee ah-lair-ZHEEK ah
phrases.cultural_note: Write serious allergies down before you travel. Key terms: les noix (nuts), le gluten, les fruits de mer (shellfish), le lait (dairy), les œufs (eggs).

responses.phonetic: dah-KOR, zhuh veh vay-ree-fyeh-RAY lay zehn-gray-DYAHN
responses.local: D'accord, je vais vérifier les ingrédients.

responses.phonetic: day-zoh-LAY, suh plah ahn kohn-TYEHN
responses.local: Désolé, ce plat en contient.

responses.english: No problem, we can adapt it.
responses.phonetic: pah duh proh-BLEM, ohn PUH ah-dap-TAY
responses.local: Pas de problème, on peut adapter.

phrases.text_local: Est-ce qu'il y a du WiFi ici ?
phrases.pronunciation: ess keel ee ah doo WEE-fee ee-SEE
phrases.cultural_note: Most French cafés offer free WiFi. The password (le mot de passe) is often on the receipt or written on a chalkboard near the counter.

responses.phonetic: wee, luh moh duh PAHS eh...
responses.local: Oui, le mot de passe est ___.

responses.phonetic: nohn, pah duh WEE-fee ee-SEE
responses.local: Non, pas de WiFi ici.

responses.phonetic: wee, seh grah-TWEE
responses.local: Oui, c'est gratuit.

phrases.text_local: Bonjour !
phrases.pronunciation: bohn-ZHOOR
phrases.cultural_note: Always say 'Bonjour' when entering any shop, café, or office — skipping it is considered rude in France. Use 'Bonsoir' after around 6pm.

responses.phonetic: bohn-ZHOOR, bohn-ZHOOR
responses.local: Bonjour, bonjour !

responses.phonetic: bohn-SWAHR
responses.local: Bonsoir !

phrases.text_local: Au revoir. Bonne journée !
phrases.pronunciation: oh ruh-VWAHR. bon zhoor-NAY
phrases.cultural_note: Always say goodbye when leaving a shop or restaurant. 'Bonne journée' (have a good day) or 'Bonne soirée' (have a good evening) is warm and appreciated.

responses.phonetic: oh ruh-VWAHR, bon zhoor-NAY
responses.local: Au revoir, bonne journée !

responses.phonetic: ah byan-TOH
responses.local: À bientôt !

phrases.text_local: Pardon ! Excusez-moi.
phrases.pronunciation: par-DOHN. ex-koo-ZAY mwah
phrases.cultural_note: 'Pardon' is used to squeeze past someone or get attention. 'Excusez-moi' is a fuller apology. Both are used constantly in everyday French life.

responses.phonetic: suh neh pah GRAHV
responses.local: Ce n'est pas grave.

responses.phonetic: pah duh soo-SEE
responses.local: Pas de souci !

phrases.text_local: C'est combien, s'il vous plaît ?
phrases.pronunciation: seh kohm-BYAHN, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: Use this for anything without a visible price tag. Pointing at the item while asking works perfectly.

responses.phonetic: seh SANK ZUH-roh
responses.local: C'est cinq euros.

responses.phonetic: luh PREE eh tah-fee-SHAY lah
responses.local: Le prix est affiché là.

phrases.text_local: Où est… s'il vous plaît ?
phrases.pronunciation: oo eh... seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: All-purpose location question. Point or show on your phone if you can't pronounce the place name.

responses.phonetic: seh par LAH
responses.local: C'est par là.

responses.phonetic: zhuh nuh seh pah, day-zoh-LAY
responses.local: Je ne sais pas, désolé.

phrases.text_local: Oui. / Non. / Peut-être.
phrases.pronunciation: WEE. / NOHN. / puh-TET-ruh
phrases.cultural_note: The French often say 'Bah oui' (well, yes) or 'Ben non' (well, no) in casual speech. 'Peut-être' is the diplomatic non-committal answer.

responses.phonetic: dah-KOR
responses.local: D'accord.

responses.phonetic: treh BYAHN
responses.local: Très bien.

phrases.text_local: Je ne sais pas.
phrases.pronunciation: zhuh nuh seh PAH
phrases.cultural_note: Simple and useful. Often followed by 'Désolé(e)' to soften it.

responses.phonetic: pah duh proh-BLEM
responses.local: Pas de problème.

responses.phonetic: zhuh veh muh rahn-say-NYAY
responses.local: Je vais me renseigner.

phrases.intent_en: Ask someone to write it down
phrases.text_local: Pouvez-vous l'écrire, s'il vous plaît ?
phrases.pronunciation: poo-VAY voo lay-KREER, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: Invaluable when you can't understand a spoken name, address, or price. Most people are happy to write things down.

responses.phonetic: byahn SOOR, vwah-LAH
responses.local: Bien sûr, voilà.

responses.english: I don't have anything to write with.
responses.phonetic: zhuh nay pah duh kwah ay-KREER
responses.local: Je n'ai pas de quoi écrire.

phrases.text_local: Un café, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: uhn ka-FAY, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: In France, 'un café' means an espresso by default. Ask for 'un café allongé' if you want a longer coffee.

responses.phonetic: too duh SWEET
responses.local: Tout de suite !

responses.phonetic: ah-VEK doo SOO-kruh
responses.local: Avec du sucre ?

responses.phonetic: voo pruh-NAY oh-truh SHOZE
responses.local: Vous prenez autre chose ?

phrases.text_local: L'addition, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: la-dee-SYOHN, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: In French cafés, the server will never bring the bill unless you ask. It's not rude — they're giving you space to enjoy your time.

responses.english: Of course, I'll bring it right away.
responses.phonetic: byan SOOR, zhuh voo lah-PORT too duh SWEET
responses.local: Bien sûr, je vous l'apporte tout de suite.

responses.english: Are you paying by card or cash?
responses.phonetic: voo pay-YAY par KART oo on es-PES
responses.local: Vous payez par carte ou en espèces ?

phrases.intent_en: Ask if a table is free
phrases.text_local: Excusez-moi, cette table est libre ?
phrases.pronunciation: ex-koo-ZAY mwah, set TAHBL eh LEEBR
phrases.cultural_note: Always greet or excuse yourself before asking. Jumping straight to a question is considered impolite.

responses.english: Yes, it's free — make yourself comfortable.
responses.phonetic: wee, el eh LEEBR, ahn-stah-LAY voo
responses.local: Oui, elle est libre, installez-vous.

responses.phonetic: day-zoh-LAY, el eh ray-zair-VAY
responses.local: Désolé, elle est réservée.

phrases.text_local: Un café crème, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: uhn ka-FAY KREM, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: A 'café crème' is the closest to a latte. Ordering a 'café au lait' is fine too but sounds more old-fashioned.

responses.phonetic: grahn oo puh-TEE
responses.local: Grand ou petit ?

responses.phonetic: too duh SWEET, vwah-LAH
responses.local: Tout de suite, voilà.

phrases.intent_en: Ask for a glass of water
phrases.text_local: Un verre d'eau, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: uhn VAIR DOH, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: Tap water is free by law in France when you order something. Just ask — no need to buy bottled water.

responses.phonetic: doo roh-bee-NEH, sah voo VAH
responses.local: Du robinet, ça vous va ?

responses.english: Do you prefer still or sparkling?
responses.phonetic: voo pray-fay-RAY PLAHT oo gah-ZUHZ
responses.local: Vous préférez plate ou gazeuse ?

phrases.text_local: Avez-vous le mot de passe WiFi ?
phrases.pronunciation: ah-VAY voo luh moh duh pahs WEE-fee
phrases.cultural_note: Most French cafés offer free WiFi. Asking politely usually results in a slip of paper with the password, or the waiter pointing to a sign on the wall.

responses.phonetic: WEE, seh kah-fay-pah-REE
responses.local: Oui, c'est 'cafeparis123'.

responses.phonetic: seh tah-fee-SHAY soor luh tah-BLOH
responses.local: C'est affiché sur le tableau.

phrases.text_local: Pour emporter, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: poor ahm-por-TAY, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: Takeaway coffee is less common in France than sitting at the bar, but city cafés and boulangeries increasingly offer it. Your coffee may come in a paper cup rather than the ceramic one used at the bar.

responses.phonetic: byahn SOOR, vwah-LAH
responses.local: Bien sûr, voilà.

responses.english: You can pay at the till.
responses.phonetic: voo poo-VAY pay-YAY ah lah KES
responses.local: Vous pouvez payer à la caisse.

phrases.text_local: Avez-vous du sucre, s'il vous plaît ?
phrases.pronunciation: ah-VAY voo doo SOO-kruh, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: Sugar is usually on the table in French cafés — either loose sachets or cubes. Sweeteners ('édulcorants') are less common; it's worth asking if you prefer them.

responses.phonetic: WEE, luh vwah-LAH
responses.local: Oui, le voilà.

responses.phonetic: seh soor lah TAHBL
responses.local: C'est sur la table.

phrases.text_local: Qu'est-ce que vous recommandez ?
phrases.pronunciation: kes-kuh voo ruh-koh-mahn-DAY
phrases.cultural_note: A great opener for pastries and snacks. The waiter will often steer you toward whatever is fresh that day — the answer is usually worth taking.

responses.phonetic: zhuh ruh-koh-MAHND luh fohn-DAHN oh shoh-koh-LAH
responses.local: Je recommande le fondant au chocolat.

responses.phonetic: lah TART doo zhoor eh day-lee-SYUHZ
responses.local: La tarte du jour est délicieuse.

phrases.text_local: Un jus d'orange pressé, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: uhn zhoo doh-RAHNZH preh-SAY, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: A 'jus pressé' (freshly squeezed) is a classic French café order and usually much better than bottled juice. It typically comes with a small straw and may be served in a carafe alongside your coffee.

responses.phonetic: treh BYAHN, sah ah-REEV
responses.local: Très bien, ça arrive.

responses.english: We've run out. A bottled juice?
responses.phonetic: noo nahn nah-VOHN ploo. uhn zhoo ahn boo-TAY
responses.local: Nous n'en avons plus. Un jus en bouteille ?

phrases.text_local: Une bière pression, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: oon bee-AIR preh-SYOHN, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: 'Pression' means draft beer. You can also say 'un demi' for a standard half-pint glass.

responses.phonetic: uhn duh-MEE oo oon PENT
responses.local: Un demi ou une pinte ?

responses.english: We have Kronenbourg and Heineken on tap.
responses.phonetic: ohn ah lah kroh-nahn-BOOR ay lah ay-neh-KEN ahn preh-SYOHN
responses.local: On a la Kronenbourg et la Heineken en pression.

responses.phonetic: vwah-LAH, bon day-gews-tah-SYOHN
responses.local: Voilà, bonne dégustation !

phrases.text_local: Un verre de vin rouge, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: uhn VAIR duh vahn ROOZH, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: Replace 'rouge' with 'blanc' (white) or 'rosé'. Ordering by the glass ('au verre') is common and affordable.

responses.english: We have a nice Bordeaux tonight.
responses.phonetic: ohn ah uhn bohn bor-DOH suh SWAHR
responses.local: On a un bon Bordeaux ce soir.

responses.english: Do you prefer something light or full-bodied?
responses.phonetic: voo pray-fay-RAY kel-kuh SHOZE duh lay-ZHAY oo duh kor-SAY
responses.local: Vous préférez quelque chose de léger ou de corsé ?

phrases.intent_en: Ask what they have on tap
phrases.text_local: Qu'est-ce que vous avez en pression ?
phrases.pronunciation: kess kuh vooz ah-VAY ahn preh-SYOHN
phrases.cultural_note: French bars often have a mix of local and international beers on tap. The bartender may point to the taps.

responses.phonetic: ohn ah lah LEF, lah say-zohn swah-sahnT-KAT-ruh ay lah gih-NES
responses.local: On a la Leffe, la 1664 et la Guinness.

responses.english: There are two beers: lager and amber.
responses.phonetic: eel ee ah DUH bee-AIR: BLOHND ay ahm-BRAY
responses.local: Il y a deux bières : blonde et ambrée.

phrases.text_local: Vous faites des cocktails ?
phrases.pronunciation: voo FET day kok-TEL
phrases.cultural_note: Not every bar makes cocktails. Traditional bars ('bars à vin') focus on wine. Look for 'bar à cocktails' for mixed drinks.

responses.phonetic: wee, vwah-LAH noh-truh KART
responses.local: Oui, voilà notre carte.

responses.english: No, we mainly do wines and beers.
responses.phonetic: nohn, ohn feh soor-TOO day VAHN ay day bee-AIR
responses.local: Non, on fait surtout des vins et des bières.

phrases.text_local: L'addition, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: la-dee-SYOHN, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: You typically pay when you leave, not per drink. Tipping is not expected but rounding up is appreciated.

responses.phonetic: sah feh dee-ZWEET ZUH-roh ahn TOO
responses.local: Ça fait dix-huit euros en tout.

responses.phonetic: voo pay-YAY par KART oo ahn lee-KEED
responses.local: Vous payez par carte ou en liquide ?

phrases.intent_en: Order another round of the same
phrases.text_local: La même chose pour tout le monde, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: lah mem SHOZE poor too luh MOHND, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: The classic way to order a round. For just yourself, shorten it to 'La même chose' or 'Un autre, s'il vous plaît.'

responses.english: Very good, I'll do the same again.
responses.phonetic: treh BYAHN, zhuh voo ruh-FEH lah mem SHOZE
responses.local: Très bien, je vous refais la même chose.

responses.english: How many people is that for?
responses.phonetic: seh kohm-BYAHN duh pair-SON
responses.local: C'est combien de personnes ?

phrases.text_local: On peut ouvrir une ardoise ?
phrases.pronunciation: ohn puh oo-VREER oon ar-DWAZ
phrases.cultural_note: Running a tab ('une ardoise', literally 'a slate') is common at wine bars and brasseries. At smaller bars, payment per drink is more typical — always ask first.

responses.phonetic: byahn SOOR, seh tah kel NOHN
responses.local: Bien sûr, c'est à quel nom ?

responses.english: We prefer to pay per round.
responses.phonetic: ohn pray-FAIR pay-YAY ah shah-kuh koh-MAHND
responses.local: On préfère payer à chaque commande.

phrases.text_local: Vous fermez à quelle heure ?
phrases.pronunciation: voo fair-MAY ah kel UR
phrases.cultural_note: French bars typically close between midnight and 2am. Nightclubs stay open later. Licensing hours vary by city — Paris bars can serve until 2am.

responses.phonetic: ohn FAIRM ah mee-NWEE
responses.local: On ferme à minuit.

responses.phonetic: ohn FAIRM ah DUH zur doo mah-TAHN
responses.local: On ferme à deux heures du matin.

phrases.text_local: Quelque chose sans alcool, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: kel-kuh SHOZE sahn zal-KOL, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: Common non-alcoholic options: Perrier, jus de fruits (juice), sirop à l'eau (flavoured syrup), diabolo menthe (mint syrup + lemonade), Orangina.

responses.english: We have juice, sparkling water, diabolo…
responses.phonetic: ohn ah doo ZHOO, duh loh gah-ZUHZ, doo dyah-BOH-loh
responses.local: On a du jus, de l'eau gazeuse, du diabolo…

responses.english: Would you like a Virgin Mojito?
responses.phonetic: voo voo-LAY uhn veer-ZHEEN moh-ZHEE-toh
responses.local: Vous voulez un Virgin Mojito ?

phrases.intent_en: Ask if they have bar snacks
phrases.text_local: Vous avez quelque chose à grignoter ?
phrases.pronunciation: vooz ah-VAY kel-kuh SHOZE ah gree-nyoh-TAY
phrases.cultural_note: Traditional French bars may offer olives, chips, or 'cacahuètes' (peanuts). Wine bars ('bars à vins') often have charcuterie or cheese plates.

responses.english: Yes, we have olives, crisps, and a charcuterie board.
responses.phonetic: wee, ohn ah day zoh-LEEV, day SHEEP ay uhn plah-TOH duh shar-koo-tuh-REE
responses.local: Oui, on a des olives, des chips et un plateau de charcuterie.

responses.phonetic: day-zoh-LAY, ohn nuh feh pah ah mahn-ZHAY
responses.local: Désolé, on ne fait pas à manger.

phrases.text_local: On peut fermer l'ardoise ?
phrases.pronunciation: ohn puh fair-MAY lar-DWAZ
phrases.cultural_note: Use this to settle your tab. The bartender will total everything up. Tipping is not expected but rounding up is appreciated.

responses.english: Of course, that's forty-two euros in total.
responses.phonetic: byahn SOOR, sah feh kah-rahnt-DUH ZUH-roh ahn TOO
responses.local: Bien sûr, ça fait quarante-deux euros en tout.

responses.phonetic: voo pay-YAY par KART oo ahn lee-KEED
responses.local: Vous payez par carte ou en liquide ?

phrases.text_local: Une bouteille de vin, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: oon boo-TAY duh VAHN, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: Ordering a bottle is usually better value than by the glass. Ask for 'vin de la maison' (house wine) for the most affordable option — often local and well-chosen.

responses.phonetic: ROOZH, BLAHN oo roh-ZAY
responses.local: Rouge, blanc ou rosé ?

responses.english: Do you have a particular budget in mind?
responses.phonetic: vooz ah-VAY uhn boo-ZHEH par-tee-koo-LYAY
responses.local: Vous avez un budget particulier ?

phrases.text_local: Bonjour !
phrases.pronunciation: bohn-ZHOOR
phrases.cultural_note: Always say 'Bonjour' when entering any shop in France. Not greeting is considered very rude.

responses.english: Hello! What can I get you?
responses.phonetic: bohn-ZHOOR! kess kuh zhuh voo SAIR
responses.local: Bonjour ! Qu'est-ce que je vous sers ?

responses.english: Hello, I'll be right with you!
responses.phonetic: bohn-ZHOOR, zhah-REEV
responses.local: Bonjour, j'arrive !

phrases.text_local: Une baguette tradition, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: oon ba-GET tra-dee-SYOHN, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: A 'baguette tradition' is made with a more traditional recipe and is generally preferred over a regular 'baguette'.

responses.phonetic: byehn KWEET oo pah troh KWEET
responses.local: Bien cuite ou pas trop cuite ?

responses.english: Here you go, it's just out of the oven.
responses.phonetic: vwah-LAH, el sor doo FOOR
responses.local: Voilà, elle sort du four.

phrases.text_local: Je voudrais un croissant, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: zhuh voo-DREH uhn kwah-SAHN, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: 'Je voudrais' (I would like) is more polite than 'Je veux' (I want). Use it in shops and restaurants.

responses.phonetic: BUHR oo nah-TOOR
responses.local: Beurre ou nature ?

responses.english: I have two left, here they are.
responses.phonetic: eel mahn REST DUH, lay vwah-LAH
responses.local: Il m'en reste deux, les voilà.

responses.phonetic: day-zoh-LAY, eel nyahn ah PLOO
responses.local: Désolé, il n'y en a plus.

phrases.text_local: C'est combien ?
phrases.pronunciation: seh kohm-BYEHN
phrases.cultural_note: Perfectly acceptable in a bakery. For more formal settings, use 'Quel est le prix ?' instead.

responses.phonetic: seh uhn ZUH-roh VAHN
responses.local: C'est un euro vingt.

responses.phonetic: DUH ZUH-roh sahn-KAHNT poor lay DUH
responses.local: Deux euros cinquante pour les deux.

phrases.text_local: Merci, au revoir !
phrases.pronunciation: mair-SEE, oh ruh-VWAHR
phrases.cultural_note: Always say goodbye when leaving a shop. 'Merci, bonne journée' (thank you, have a good day) is even friendlier.

responses.english: Thank you, have a good day!
responses.phonetic: mair-SEE ah voo, bon zhoor-NAY
responses.local: Merci à vous, bonne journée !

responses.phonetic: oh ruh-VWAHR, bon day-gews-tah-SYOHN
responses.local: Au revoir, bonne dégustation !

phrases.text_local: Deux de ceux-ci, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: duh duh suh-SEE, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: Pointing at the item and saying this is entirely normal in a French boulangerie. If you're choosing from a display case, you can also say 'celui-là' (that one there) and hold up two fingers.

responses.phonetic: duh krwah-SAHN, seh SAH
responses.local: Deux croissants, c'est ça ?

responses.phonetic: vwah-LAH, duh ZUH-roh
responses.local: Voilà, deux euros.

phrases.text_local: Qu'est-ce qui est frais aujourd'hui ?
phrases.pronunciation: kes kee eh FREH oh-zhoor-DWEE
phrases.cultural_note: French boulangeries bake several times a day. This question often results in a recommendation for whatever just came out of the oven. Morning visits are best for croissants and pain au chocolat — later in the day, look for fresh tarts or savoury quiches.

responses.english: The country loaf will be out of the oven in ten minutes.
responses.phonetic: luh pahn duh kahm-PAN-yuh sor doo FOOR dahn dee mee-NOOT
responses.local: Le pain de campagne sort du four dans dix minutes.

responses.phonetic: noh krwah-SAHN vyehn ZHOOS duh sor-TEER
responses.local: Nos croissants viennent juste de sortir.

phrases.intent_en: Order a ham and butter sandwich
phrases.text_local: Un jambon-beurre, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: uhn zhahm-BON BUR, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: The jambon-beurre (ham and butter baguette) is France's most popular sandwich and a cornerstone of boulangerie culture. It is simple, perfect, and deeply French. Most boulangeries make them to order — specify if you want mustard ('moutarde') or gherkins ('cornichons').

responses.phonetic: ah-VEK duh lah moo-TARD
responses.local: Avec de la moutarde ?

responses.phonetic: soor kel sort duh PAN
responses.local: Sur quelle sorte de pain ?

phrases.intent_en: Ask if there are gluten-free options
phrases.text_local: Avez-vous des produits sans gluten ?
phrases.pronunciation: ah-VAY voo day proh-DWEE sahn gloo-TEN
phrases.cultural_note: Dedicated gluten-free boulangeries are rare in France, but some larger boulangeries and supermarkets stock a small range of gluten-free breads and pastries ('sans gluten'). It's always worth asking — and showing the written phrase helps if your pronunciation is uncertain.

responses.phonetic: WEE, noo zah-VOHN doo pahn sahn gloo-TEN
responses.local: Oui, nous avons du pain sans gluten.

responses.english: No, I'm sorry, we don't have any.
responses.phonetic: NOHN, zhuh swee day-zoh-LAY, noo nahn nah-VOHN pah
responses.local: Non, je suis désolé, nous n'en avons pas.

phrases.text_local: Bonjour, une table pour deux, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: bohn-ZHOOR, oon TAHBL poor DUH, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: Always greet with 'Bonjour' before making any request. Specify the number of guests.

responses.phonetic: wee, par ee-SEE seel voo PLEH
responses.local: Oui, par ici s'il vous plaît.

responses.phonetic: vooz ah-VAY oon ray-zair-vah-SYOHN
responses.local: Vous avez une réservation ?

responses.phonetic: day-zoh-LAY, noo SOM kohm-PLEH suh SWAHR
responses.local: Désolé, nous sommes complets ce soir.

phrases.text_local: La carte, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: lah KART, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: In France, 'le menu' usually means a fixed-price set meal. 'La carte' is the full à-la-carte menu.

responses.phonetic: vwah-LAH lah KART
responses.local: Voilà la carte.

responses.english: We also have a set menu today for fifteen euros.
responses.phonetic: vooz ah-VAY oh-SEE uhn muh-NOO doo ZHOOR ah KANZ ZUH-roh
responses.local: Vous avez aussi un menu du jour à quinze euros.

phrases.intent_en: Ask for the dish of the day
phrases.text_local: Quel est le plat du jour ?
phrases.pronunciation: kel eh luh PLAH doo ZHOOR
phrases.cultural_note: The 'plat du jour' is usually the freshest, best-value option and changes daily.

responses.phonetic: oh-zhoor-DWEE seh uhn mah-GREH duh kah-NAR
responses.local: Aujourd'hui c'est un magret de canard.

responses.english: We have grilled salmon with vegetables.
responses.phonetic: ohn ah uhn soh-MOHN gree-YAY ah-VEK day lay-GOOM
responses.local: On a un saumon grillé avec des légumes.

responses.phonetic: sah vooz ahn-tay-RES
responses.local: Ça vous intéresse ?

phrases.text_local: Je suis allergique aux noix.
phrases.pronunciation: zhuh swee ah-lair-ZHEEK oh NWAH
phrases.cultural_note: Replace 'noix' with your specific allergen. Common ones: gluten, lait (milk), œufs (eggs).

responses.english: OK, I'll check with the kitchen.
responses.phonetic: dah-KOR, zhuh veh vay-ree-FYAY ah-VEK lah kwee-ZEEN
responses.local: D'accord, je vais vérifier avec la cuisine.

responses.english: This dish contains nuts, I'd advise against it.
responses.phonetic: suh PLAH kohn-TYEHN day NWAH, zhuh voo day-kohn-SAY
responses.local: Ce plat contient des noix, je vous déconseille.

responses.english: No problem, we can adapt it.
responses.phonetic: pah duh proh-BLEM, ohn PUH ah-dahp-TAY
responses.local: Pas de problème, on peut adapter.

phrases.text_local: Une carafe d'eau, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: oon ka-RAF DOH, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: Tap water is free in restaurants. Ask for 'une carafe d'eau' — they'll bring a pitcher. No need to buy bottled.

responses.phonetic: byan SOOR, too duh SWEET
responses.local: Bien sûr, tout de suite.

responses.phonetic: voo pray-fay-RAY duh LOH ahn boo-TAY
responses.local: Vous préférez de l'eau en bouteille ?

phrases.text_local: L'addition, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: la-dee-SYOHN, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: The server won't bring the bill until you ask. Tipping is not expected — service is included ('service compris').

responses.english: Of course, I'll bring it right away.
responses.phonetic: byan SOOR, zhuh voo lah-PORT too duh SWEET
responses.local: Bien sûr, je vous l'apporte tout de suite.

responses.english: Are you paying together or separately?
responses.phonetic: voo pay-YAY ahn-SAHM-bluh oo say-pah-ray-MAHN
responses.local: Vous payez ensemble ou séparément ?

responses.phonetic: seh KAT-ruh-vahn-DUH ZUH-roh
responses.local: C'est quatre-vingt-deux euros.

phrases.text_local: Je suis végétarien.
phrases.pronunciation: zhuh swee vay-zhay-tah-RYEHN
phrases.cultural_note: Add 'ne' for feminine: 'végétarienne'. For vegan, say 'végan'. France is increasingly vegetarian-friendly.

responses.english: No problem, we have several options.
responses.phonetic: pah duh proh-BLEM, ohn ah ploo-ZYUHR op-SYOHN
responses.local: Pas de problème, on a plusieurs options.

responses.english: Here are the dishes without meat.
responses.phonetic: vwah-SEE lay PLAH sahn VYAHND
responses.local: Voici les plats sans viande.

responses.phonetic: voo mahn-ZHAY doo pwah-SOHN
responses.local: Vous mangez du poisson ?

phrases.intent_en: Say you're still looking at the menu
phrases.text_local: Nous regardons encore la carte, merci.
phrases.pronunciation: noo ruh-gar-DOHN ahn-KOR lah KART, mair-SEE
phrases.cultural_note: Say this if the waiter comes to take your order too soon. French waiters appreciate the signal and will give you space without hovering.

responses.phonetic: byahn SOOR, pruh-NAY voh-truh TAHN
responses.local: Bien sûr, prenez votre temps.

responses.english: OK, I'll be back in a few minutes.
responses.phonetic: dah-KOR, zhuh ruh-VYAHN dahn kel-kuh mee-NOOT
responses.local: D'accord, je reviens dans quelques minutes.

phrases.text_local: Nous sommes prêts à commander.
phrases.pronunciation: noo SOM preh ah koh-mahn-DAY
phrases.cultural_note: Make eye contact with your waiter — saying 'Monsieur !' or 'Madame !' is perfectly acceptable in France to get attention.

responses.phonetic: byahn SOOR, zhuh vooz ay-KOOT
responses.local: Bien sûr, je vous écoute.

responses.phonetic: zhah-REEV too duh SWEET
responses.local: J'arrive tout de suite !

phrases.text_local: Qu'est-ce que vous recommandez ?
phrases.pronunciation: kess kuh voo ruh-koh-mahn-DAY
phrases.cultural_note: French waiters are proud of their menu and give genuine recommendations. This often leads to the freshest or most popular dish of the day.

responses.english: I recommend the duck breast — it's our speciality.
responses.phonetic: zhuh voo kohn-SAY luh mah-GREH duh kah-NAR, seh noh-truh spay-see-ah-lee-TAY
responses.local: Je vous conseille le magret de canard, c'est notre spécialité.

responses.english: The dish of the day is excellent tonight.
responses.phonetic: luh PLAH doo ZHOOR eh tex-seh-LAHN suh SWAHR
responses.local: Le plat du jour est excellent ce soir.

phrases.text_local: C'est quoi exactement, ce plat ?
phrases.pronunciation: seh KWAH eg-zak-tuh-MAHN, suh PLAH
phrases.cultural_note: Perfectly fine to ask. French waiters know their menu well and will describe ingredients and preparation in detail.

responses.english: It's slow-cooked beef with carrots and potatoes.
responses.phonetic: seh doo BUF mee-zhoh-TAY ah-VEK day kah-ROT ay day pom duh TAIR
responses.local: C'est du bœuf mijoté avec des carottes et des pommes de terre.

responses.english: It's a regional speciality, very flavourful.
responses.phonetic: seh toon spay-see-ah-lee-TAY duh lah ray-ZHOHN, treh sah-voo-RUH
responses.local: C'est une spécialité de la région, très savoureux.

phrases.intent_en: Ask if a dish is spicy
phrases.text_local: Ce plat est épicé ?
phrases.pronunciation: suh PLAH eh tay-pee-SAY
phrases.cultural_note: Traditional French cuisine is rarely spicy. Look out for 'piment' or 'harissa' on the menu — those are the main clues a dish has heat.

responses.phonetic: nohn, seh treh DOO
responses.local: Non, c'est très doux.

responses.english: Yes, there's a little chilli but it's not too strong.
responses.phonetic: wee, eel ee ah uhn PUH duh pee-MAHN, meh suh neh pah troh FOR
responses.local: Oui, il y a un peu de piment, mais ce n'est pas trop fort.

phrases.intent_en: Ask for a dish without an ingredient
phrases.text_local: Sans [ingrédient], s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: sahn [...], seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: Swap [ingrédient] for: oignons (onions/ohn-YOHN), ail (garlic/AY), fromage (cheese/froh-MAZH), crème (cream/KREM), sel (salt/SEL). French kitchens will try to accommodate.

responses.english: No problem, I'll make a note of it.
responses.phonetic: pah duh proh-BLEM, zhuh veh luh noh-TAY
responses.local: Pas de problème, je vais le noter.

responses.phonetic: zhuh veh vay-ree-FYAY ah-VEK lah kwee-ZEEN
responses.local: Je vais vérifier avec la cuisine.

phrases.text_local: On peut payer séparément ?
phrases.pronunciation: ohn puh pay-YAY say-pah-ray-MAHN
phrases.cultural_note: Splitting bills is less common in France than some countries but entirely possible. Ask before the check arrives to make it easier for the server.

responses.english: Of course, I'll make separate bills.
responses.phonetic: byahn SOOR, zhuh veh FAIR day za-dee-SYOHN say-pah-RAY
responses.local: Bien sûr, je vais faire des additions séparées.

responses.english: How much is each person paying?
responses.phonetic: voo pay-YAY kohm-BYAHN shah-CUHN
responses.local: Vous payez combien chacun ?

phrases.intent_en: Ask to split the bill evenly
phrases.text_local: On partage en parts égales, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: ohn par-TAZH ahn par ay-GAL, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: The waiter will calculate — you don't need to do the maths. You can also say 'On fait moitié-moitié' (we split it half and half).

responses.phonetic: dah-KOR, sah feh VAHN ZUH-roh shah-CUHN
responses.local: D'accord, ça fait vingt euros chacun.

responses.english: Very good, I'll sort that out for you.
responses.phonetic: treh BYAHN, zhuh voo pray-PAR sah
responses.local: Très bien, je vous prépare ça.

phrases.text_local: Tout sur une seule addition, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: too soor oon suhl ah-dee-SYOHN, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: Use this when one person is treating the table. Say it clearly before anyone else tries to pay — it saves the awkward back-and-forth.

responses.english: Very good, I'll bring the bill.
responses.phonetic: treh BYAHN, zhuh vooz ah-PORT lah-dee-SYOHN
responses.local: Très bien, je vous apporte l'addition.

responses.phonetic: dah-KOR, seh tahn-SAHM-bluh
responses.local: D'accord, c'est ensemble.

phrases.text_local: Vous avez des boîtes pour emporter ?
phrases.pronunciation: vooz ah-VAY day BWAHT poor ahm-por-TAY
phrases.cultural_note: Doggy bags are becoming more common in France thanks to anti-food-waste legislation. Smaller or traditional restaurants may not always have them — worth asking.

responses.phonetic: wee, zhuh vooz ahn ah-PORT oon
responses.local: Oui, je vous en apporte une.

responses.phonetic: day-zoh-LAY, noo nahn nah-VOHN pah
responses.local: Désolé, nous n'en avons pas.

phrases.text_local: Vous avez une table en terrasse ?
phrases.pronunciation: vooz ah-VAY oon TAHBL ahn teh-RAS
phrases.cultural_note: Terrasse seating is hugely popular in France. Arrive early or reserve ahead — terrace spots fill fast on warm days.

responses.phonetic: wee, par ee-SEE seel voo PLEH
responses.local: Oui, par ici s'il vous plaît.

responses.phonetic: day-zoh-LAY, lah teh-RAS eh kohm-PLET
responses.local: Désolé, la terrasse est complète.

phrases.text_local: Je préfère à l'intérieur, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: zhuh pray-FAIR ah lahn-tay-RYUR, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: Useful when it's too cold, sunny, or noisy outside. Indoor seating is often quieter and better for conversation.

responses.phonetic: byahn SOOR, swee-VAY mwah
responses.local: Bien sûr, suivez-moi.

responses.english: We have a nice table by the window.
responses.phonetic: ohn ah oon BEL TAHBL preh duh lah fuh-NET
responses.local: On a une belle table près de la fenêtre.

phrases.intent_en: Say you have no reservation and ask about space
phrases.text_local: On n'a pas de réservation — vous avez de la place ?
phrases.pronunciation: ohn nah pah duh ray-zair-vah-SYOHN — vooz ah-VAY duh lah PLAS
phrases.cultural_note: Best chances without a reservation: arrive right at opening (noon or 7:30pm), or try mid-week. Popular Parisian restaurants fill up fast on weekends.

responses.english: Yes, I have a table for you.
responses.phonetic: wee, zhay oon TAHBL poor voo
responses.local: Oui, j'ai une table pour vous.

responses.english: Sorry, we're fully booked. Shall we call you if there's a cancellation?
responses.phonetic: day-zoh-LAY, noo SOM kohm-PLEH. voo voo-LAY kohn voo zah-PEL seel ee ah uhn day-zees-tuh-MAHN
responses.local: Désolé, nous sommes complets. Vous voulez qu'on vous appelle s'il y a un désistement ?

phrases.text_local: Vous pouvez nous apporter encore du pain ?
phrases.pronunciation: voo poo-VAY nooz ah-por-TAY ahn-KOR doo PAHN
phrases.cultural_note: Bread is free and always refillable in French restaurants. Never hesitate to ask — it's expected and part of the experience.

responses.phonetic: byahn SOOR, too duh SWEET
responses.local: Bien sûr, tout de suite.

responses.phonetic: vwah-LAH doo PAHN FREH
responses.local: Voilà du pain frais.

phrases.intent_en: Say this isn't what you ordered
phrases.text_local: Ce n'est pas ce que j'ai commandé.
phrases.pronunciation: suh neh pah suh kuh zhay koh-mahn-DAY
phrases.cultural_note: Say this calmly — mistakes happen. Point to the correct dish on the menu if needed. French staff will correct errors promptly.

responses.english: I'm very sorry, I'll correct that immediately.
responses.phonetic: zhuh swee day-zoh-LAY, zhuh veh koh-ree-ZHAY sah ee-may-dyah-tuh-MAHN
responses.local: Je suis désolé, je vais corriger ça immédiatement.

responses.english: Excuse me, let me check your order.
responses.phonetic: ex-koo-ZAY mwah, zhuh vay-ree-FEE voh-truh koh-MAHND
responses.local: Excusez-moi, je vérifie votre commande.

phrases.text_local: Où est l'enregistrement, s'il vous plaît ?
phrases.pronunciation: oo eh lahn-reh-zhees-truh-MAHN, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: French airports use 'enregistrement' for check-in. Signs may also say 'Comptoirs d'enregistrement'.

responses.english: It's at terminal two, level one.
responses.phonetic: seh oh tair-mee-NAL DUH, nee-VOH UHN
responses.local: C'est au terminal deux, niveau un.

responses.phonetic: swee-VAY lay pah-NOH day-PAR
responses.local: Suivez les panneaux 'Départs'.

phrases.text_local: Où est la porte d'embarquement ?
phrases.pronunciation: oo eh lah PORT dahm-bark-MAHN
phrases.cultural_note: 'Porte' means gate. Boarding is called 'embarquement'. Screens showing gates are 'écrans d'affichage'.

responses.english: Gate K41 is at the end of the corridor on the right.
responses.phonetic: lah PORT kah kah-RAHNT-ahn-UHN eh oh BOO doo koo-LWAHR ah DRWAHT
responses.local: La porte K41 est au bout du couloir à droite.

responses.english: Check the screens — the gate may change.
responses.phonetic: vay-ree-FYAY soor lay zay-KRAHN, lah PORT puh shahn-ZHAY
responses.local: Vérifiez sur les écrans, la porte peut changer.

phrases.text_local: Ma valise n'est pas arrivée.
phrases.pronunciation: mah vah-LEEZ neh pah ah-ree-VAY
phrases.cultural_note: Head to 'Livraison des bagages' (baggage claim) and find the airline desk. Have your luggage tag ready.

responses.english: Do you have your flight number and baggage tag?
responses.phonetic: vooz ah-VAY voh-truh noo-MAY-roh duh VOL ay lay-tee-KET duh ba-GAZH
responses.local: Vous avez votre numéro de vol et l'étiquette de bagage ?

responses.english: Fill in this form — we'll track down your bag.
responses.phonetic: rahn-plee-SAY suh for-moo-LAIR, ohn vah ruh-troo-VAY voh-truh vah-LEEZ
responses.local: Remplissez ce formulaire, on va retrouver votre valise.

phrases.intent_en: Ask how to get to the city center
phrases.text_local: Comment aller au centre-ville ?
phrases.pronunciation: koh-MAHN ah-LAY oh SAHN-truh VEEL
phrases.cultural_note: From CDG: RER B train to Paris (~35 min). From Orly: Orlyval + RER B or tramway T7. Cheaper than taxis.

responses.english: Take the RER B — it's the fastest.
responses.phonetic: pruh-NAY luh air-uh-air BAY, seh luh ploo rah-PEED
responses.local: Prenez le RER B, c'est le plus rapide.

responses.english: There's also the shuttle — it's more comfortable.
responses.phonetic: eel ee ah oh-SEE lah nah-VET, seh ploo kohn-for-TAHBL
responses.local: Il y a aussi la navette, c'est plus confortable.

responses.english: By taxi expect around fifty euros.
responses.phonetic: ahn tak-SEE kohm-TAY ahn-vee-ROHN sahn-KAHNT ZUH-roh
responses.local: En taxi comptez environ cinquante euros.

phrases.text_local: Le vol est en retard ?
phrases.pronunciation: luh VOL eh tahn ruh-TAR
phrases.cultural_note: 'Retardé' means delayed, 'annulé' means cancelled. Check the 'écrans d'affichage' (departure screens) for updates.

responses.english: Yes, it's delayed by one hour.
responses.phonetic: wee, eel ah oon UHR duh ruh-TAR
responses.local: Oui, il a une heure de retard.

responses.english: For now it's on time, but keep an eye on the screens.
responses.phonetic: poor lahn-STAHN eel eh tah LUHR, meh soor-vay-YAY lay zay-KRAHN
responses.local: Pour l'instant il est à l'heure, mais surveillez les écrans.

phrases.text_local: Un billet pour Lyon, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: uhn bee-YEH poor lee-OHN, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: Replace 'Lyon' with your destination. 'Aller simple' means one-way; 'aller-retour' means round-trip.

responses.phonetic: ah-LAY SAMPL oo ah-LAY ruh-TOOR
responses.local: Aller simple ou aller-retour ?

responses.phonetic: poor kel DAT
responses.local: Pour quelle date ?

responses.phonetic: pruh-MYAIR oo DUH-zyem KLASS
responses.local: Première ou deuxième classe ?

phrases.text_local: C'est quel quai, s'il vous plaît ?
phrases.pronunciation: seh kel KAY, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: 'Quai' means platform. Departure boards are called 'tableau des départs'.

responses.phonetic: seh luh KAY noo-MAY-roh SANK
responses.local: C'est le quai numéro cinq.

responses.phonetic: ruh-gar-DAY luh tah-BLOH day day-PAR
responses.local: Regardez le tableau des départs.

phrases.intent_en: Ask if the train is on time
phrases.text_local: Le train est à l'heure ?
phrases.pronunciation: luh TREHN eh tah LUHR
phrases.cultural_note: 'En retard' means delayed. French trains (TGV/TER) are generally punctual.

responses.phonetic: wee, eel eh tah LUHR
responses.local: Oui, il est à l'heure.

responses.phonetic: eel ah dee mee-NOOT duh ruh-TAR
responses.local: Il a dix minutes de retard.

phrases.intent_en: Confirm this is the right train
phrases.text_local: C'est bien le train pour Lyon ?
phrases.pronunciation: seh byehn luh TREHN poor lee-OHN
phrases.cultural_note: Replace 'Lyon' with your destination. Ask anyone on the platform — people are usually happy to confirm.

responses.phonetic: wee, seh byehn SAH
responses.local: Oui, c'est bien ça.

responses.english: No, the Lyon train is the next one.
responses.phonetic: nohn, luh TREHN poor lee-OHN seh luh swee-VAHN
responses.local: Non, le train pour Lyon c'est le suivant.

phrases.intent_en: Ask where to validate your ticket
phrases.text_local: Où est-ce que je composte mon billet ?
phrases.pronunciation: oo ess kuh zhuh kohm-POST mohn bee-YEH
phrases.cultural_note: Regional TER tickets must be composted (validated) before boarding. Look for yellow machines on the platform.

responses.english: The yellow machines are on the platform.
responses.phonetic: lay mah-SHEEN ZHOHN sohn soor luh KAY
responses.local: Les machines jaunes sont sur le quai.

responses.english: Do you have a TGV ticket? No need to validate.
responses.phonetic: vooz ah-VAY uhn bee-YEH tay-zhay-VAY? pah buh-ZWAHN duh kohm-pos-TAY
responses.local: Vous avez un billet TGV ? Pas besoin de composter.

phrases.text_local: Un aller-retour pour…, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: uhn ah-LAY ruh-TOOR poor..., seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: Return tickets on French TGV and intercity trains often work out cheaper than two singles — especially if booked in advance. 'Aller-retour' is always the right phrase. Specify the return date and time if you know it.

responses.english: When would you like to return?
responses.phonetic: poor KAHN sweh-TAY voo rahn-TRAY
responses.local: Pour quand souhaitez-vous rentrer ?

responses.phonetic: ahn pruh-MYAIR oo ahn suh-KOHND KLAHS
responses.local: En première ou en seconde classe ?

phrases.intent_en: Ask if seat reservation is required
phrases.text_local: Est-ce qu'il faut une réservation de place ?
phrases.pronunciation: es keel foh oon ray-zehr-vah-SYOHN duh PLAHS
phrases.cultural_note: On TGV and Intercités trains in France, seat reservation is compulsory and included in the ticket price. On regional TER trains, it is not required and seats are first-come, first-served. Always check before boarding.

responses.english: Yes, reservation is compulsory on this train.
responses.phonetic: WEE, lah ray-zehr-vah-SYOHN eh toh-blee-gah-TWAHR soor suh TRAHN
responses.local: Oui, la réservation est obligatoire sur ce train.

responses.phonetic: NOHN, voo poo-VAY voo zahn-stah-LAY lee-bruh-MAHN
responses.local: Non, vous pouvez vous installer librement.

phrases.intent_en: Ask where the left-luggage lockers are
phrases.text_local: Où sont les consignes automatiques ?
phrases.pronunciation: oo sohn lay kohn-SEEN-yuh oh-toh-mah-TEEK
phrases.cultural_note: Most major French stations have automated luggage lockers ('consignes automatiques'). They accept cards and charge by the hour. Security restrictions mean some stations have limited or no consigne service — it's worth checking before you arrive.

responses.phonetic: lay kohn-SEEN-yuh sohn toh nee-VOH mwahn-UHN
responses.local: Les consignes sont au niveau -1.

responses.english: There are no lockers in this station.
responses.phonetic: eel nyah pah duh kohn-SEEN-yuh dahn set GAR
responses.local: Il n'y a pas de consignes dans cette gare.

phrases.intent_en: Ask to see the timetable for the next trains
phrases.text_local: Quels sont les prochains trains pour… ?
phrases.pronunciation: kel sohn lay proh-SHAHN TRAHN poor
phrases.cultural_note: Real-time departure boards ('tableau des départs') are displayed throughout French stations. The SNCF app and website also show live train times. Intercity trains often require advance booking, especially on popular routes at peak times.

responses.english: The next one leaves in twenty minutes, platform 7.
responses.phonetic: luh proh-SHAHN par dahn VAHN mee-NOOT, vwah SET
responses.local: Le prochain part dans vingt minutes, voie 7.

responses.english: Look at the departures board over there.
responses.phonetic: ruh-gar-DAY luh tah-BLOH day day-PAR lah-BAH
responses.local: Regardez le tableau des départs là-bas.

phrases.intent_en: Tell the staff about a connection you need to make
phrases.text_local: J'ai une correspondance à… à… heures.
phrases.pronunciation: zhay oon koh-reh-spohn-DAHNS ah..., ah... UR
phrases.cultural_note: French train staff can check your connection in real time and may issue a new ticket if your train is delayed and you'll miss it. The rule is: if you hold a reservation and your train is delayed by more than 30 minutes, SNCF owes you compensation.

responses.phonetic: nuh voo zan-kyeh-TAY pah, voo zah-VAY luh TAHN
responses.local: Ne vous inquiétez pas, vous avez le temps.

responses.english: If the train is late, let the conductor know.
responses.phonetic: see luh TRAHN eh tahn ruh-TAR, pray-vuh-NAY luh kohn-troh-LUR
responses.local: Si le train est en retard, prévenez le contrôleur.

phrases.text_local: Quelle ligne pour aller à Montmartre ?
phrases.pronunciation: kel LEEN-yuh poor ah-LAY ah mohn-MARTR
phrases.cultural_note: Replace 'Montmartre' with your destination. Parisians are usually helpful if you look at the map and ask.

responses.phonetic: pruh-NAY lah LEEN-yuh DUH, dee-rek-SYOHN nah-SYOHN
responses.local: Prenez la ligne deux, direction Nation.

responses.english: It's direct — no need to change.
responses.phonetic: seh dee-REKT, pah buh-ZWAHN duh shahn-ZHAY
responses.local: C'est direct, pas besoin de changer.

phrases.intent_en: Ask where to buy a ticket
phrases.text_local: Où est-ce que je peux acheter un ticket ?
phrases.pronunciation: oo ess kuh zhuh PUH ash-TAY uhn tee-KAY
phrases.cultural_note: Paris uses the Navigo Easy card (rechargeable). Paper tickets are being phased out. Buy at machines or counters.

responses.english: There are ticket machines right over there.
responses.phonetic: eel ee ah day BORN oh-toh-mah-TEEK zhoos LAH
responses.local: Il y a des bornes automatiques juste là.

responses.english: You can also buy online on the app.
responses.phonetic: voo poo-VAY oh-SEE ash-TAY ahn LEEN-yuh soor lah-PLEE
responses.local: Vous pouvez aussi acheter en ligne sur l'appli.

phrases.intent_en: Ask if this is the right direction
phrases.text_local: C'est bien la direction La Défense ?
phrases.pronunciation: seh byehn lah dee-rek-SYOHN lah day-FAHNSS
phrases.cultural_note: Metro lines are identified by their end stations. Check the terminus name on the platform to confirm direction.

responses.phonetic: wee, seh lah BON dee-rek-SYOHN
responses.local: Oui, c'est la bonne direction.

responses.english: No, you need the platform on the other side.
responses.phonetic: nohn, eel foh prahndr luh KAY dahn FAHSS
responses.local: Non, il faut prendre le quai d'en face.

phrases.intent_en: Ask where the next stop is
phrases.text_local: C'est quoi le prochain arrêt ?
phrases.pronunciation: seh kwah luh proh-SHEHN ah-REH
phrases.cultural_note: Most metros announce stops automatically. On buses, press the red 'Arrêt demandé' button before your stop.

responses.phonetic: luh proh-SHEHN ah-REH seh oh-pay-RAH
responses.local: Le prochain arrêt c'est Opéra.

responses.english: In two stops you'll be at Châtelet.
responses.phonetic: dahn DUH stah-SYOHN vooz ah-ree-VAY ah SHAH-tleh
responses.local: Dans deux stations vous arrivez à Châtelet.

phrases.intent_en: Confirm this metro goes to your stop
phrases.text_local: Ce métro va bien à Châtelet ?
phrases.pronunciation: suh MAY-troh vah byehn ah SHAH-tleh
phrases.cultural_note: Replace 'Châtelet' with your stop. A nod or 'oui' is all you need. Check the line terminus on platform signs.

responses.phonetic: wee, seh dahn TRWAH ah-REH
responses.local: Oui, c'est dans trois arrêts.

responses.english: No, get off and take line 4.
responses.phonetic: nohn, day-sahn-DAY ay pruh-NAY lah LEEN-yuh KATR
responses.local: Non, descendez et prenez la ligne quatre.

phrases.intent_en: Ask about a day pass or book of tickets
phrases.text_local: Y a-t-il un forfait journalier ou un carnet ?
phrases.pronunciation: ee ah-TEEL uhn for-FAY zhoor-nah-LYAY oo uhn kar-NAY
phrases.cultural_note: Paris metro tickets are now on a card-based system (Navigo Easy). A 'carnet' (book of 10 single tickets) offers a small discount over singles. A 'Navigo Jour' (day pass) covers all zones and is excellent value for tourists exploring beyond the centre.

responses.english: Yes, a day pass costs 14 euros.
responses.phonetic: WEE, uhn for-FAY zhoor-nah-LYAY koo KATORZ ZUH-roh
responses.local: Oui, un forfait journalier coûte 14 euros.

responses.english: The book of tickets is available here.
responses.phonetic: luh kar-NAY eh dees-poh-NEE-bluh ee-SEE
responses.local: Le carnet est disponible ici.

phrases.intent_en: Ask where to change / transfer trains
phrases.text_local: Où est la correspondance pour la ligne… ?
phrases.pronunciation: oo eh lah koh-reh-spohn-DAHNS poor lah LEEN-yuh
phrases.cultural_note: Paris has 16 metro lines with many interchange points ('correspondances'). Follow the orange signs for transfers inside the station — you don't need to exit and re-enter. Major interchanges like Châtelet–Les Halles connect up to 5 lines.

responses.phonetic: lah koh-reh-spohn-DAHNS eh tah sha-TLEH
responses.local: La correspondance est à Châtelet.

responses.phonetic: swee-VAY lay pah-NOH oh-RAHNZH
responses.local: Suivez les panneaux orange.

phrases.intent_en: Ask if there is an elevator / lift
phrases.text_local: Y a-t-il un ascenseur dans cette station ?
phrases.pronunciation: ee ah-TEEL uhn ah-sahn-SUR dahn set stah-SYOHN
phrases.cultural_note: Accessibility varies widely in Paris. Many older stations have no elevator and only stairs or escalators. The newer lines (14, 15, 16) are fully accessible. The RATP website and app show which stations have lifts.

responses.english: Yes, it's at the end of the platform.
responses.phonetic: WEE, eel eh toh boo doo KAY
responses.local: Oui, il est au bout du quai.

responses.phonetic: NOHN, eel nyah kuh day zes-kahl-YAY
responses.local: Non, il n'y a que des escaliers.

phrases.intent_en: Ask how many stops to your destination
phrases.text_local: C'est combien d'arrêts jusqu'à… ?
phrases.pronunciation: seh kohm-BYAHN dah-REH zhoos-KAH
phrases.cultural_note: Counting stops is the easiest way to navigate without reading every sign. Other passengers are usually very happy to help — just point to the stop on the map as you ask.

responses.phonetic: seh TWAH zah-REH
responses.local: C'est trois arrêts.

responses.english: You need to change at République.
responses.phonetic: voo duh-VAY shahn-ZHAY ah ray-poo-BLEEK
responses.local: Vous devez changer à République.

phrases.intent_en: Ask which exit to take for a street or landmark
phrases.text_local: Quelle sortie pour… s'il vous plaît ?
phrases.pronunciation: kel sor-TEE poor... seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: Paris metro stations often have multiple exits. Taking the right one saves you from surfacing on the wrong side of a junction. The exits are labelled with street names — just show the station name on your phone if needed.

responses.phonetic: sor-TEE noo-may-ROH TWAH, roo duh ree-voh-LEE
responses.local: Sortie numéro 3, rue de Rivoli.

responses.english: Follow the signs for the Eiffel Tower.
responses.phonetic: swee-VAY lay pah-NOH poor lah TOOR ay-FEL
responses.local: Suivez les panneaux pour la Tour Eiffel.

phrases.text_local: Bonjour, j'ai une réservation au nom de…
phrases.pronunciation: bohn-ZHOOR, zhay oon ray-zer-vah-SYOHN oh NOHM duh…
phrases.cultural_note: Have your booking confirmation ready. Most hotels will ask for your passport at check-in.

responses.phonetic: VOH-truh pass-POR, seel voo PLEH
responses.local: Votre passeport, s'il vous plaît.

responses.english: Your room is 203, on the second floor.
responses.phonetic: VOH-truh SHAHMBRUH eh lah DUH sahn TRWAH, oh DUH-zyem ay-TAZH
responses.local: Votre chambre est la deux cent trois, au deuxième étage.

responses.phonetic: luh puh-TEE day-zhuh-NAY eh sair-VEE duh SET UHR ah DEE UHR
responses.local: Le petit-déjeuner est servi de sept heures à dix heures.

phrases.text_local: Quel est le mot de passe du Wi-Fi ?
phrases.pronunciation: kel eh luh MOH duh PASS doo wee-FEE
phrases.cultural_note: Wi-Fi is pronounced 'wee-fee' in French, not 'why-fye'.

responses.english: The password is written on this card.
responses.phonetic: luh MOH duh PASS eh ay-KREE soor set KART
responses.local: Le mot de passe est écrit sur cette carte.

responses.phonetic: seh pah-REE duh-meel-vahn-vahn-KAT, too tahn mee-noos-KOOL
responses.local: C'est 'Paris2024', tout en minuscules.

phrases.text_local: Est-ce qu'un départ tardif est possible ?
phrases.pronunciation: ess kuh uhn day-PAR tar-DEEF eh poh-SEEBL
phrases.cultural_note: Standard checkout is usually 11:00. Ask politely — many hotels will accommodate if rooms are available.

responses.english: Yes, we can keep you until noon at no extra charge.
responses.phonetic: wee, ohn puh voo gar-DAY zhoos-KAH mee-DEE sahn soo-play-MAHN
responses.local: Oui, on peut vous garder jusqu'à midi sans supplément.

responses.english: Sorry, the room is booked from this morning.
responses.phonetic: day-zoh-LAY, lah SHAHMBRUH eh ray-zair-VAY suh mah-TAHN
responses.local: Désolé, la chambre est réservée ce matin.

responses.english: A late checkout at 2pm costs twenty euros.
responses.phonetic: uhn day-PAR tar-DEEF ah kah-TORZ UHR koot VAHN ZUH-roh
responses.local: Un départ tardif à quatorze heures coûte vingt euros.

phrases.text_local: Le petit-déjeuner est à quelle heure ?
phrases.pronunciation: luh puh-TEE day-zhuh-NAY eh tah kel UHR
phrases.cultural_note: Hotel breakfast is usually 7:00–10:00. 'Petit-déjeuner inclus' means breakfast is included in your rate.

responses.english: From 7 to 10, dining room on the ground floor.
responses.phonetic: duh SET UHR ah DEE ZUHr, sal oh ray-duh-shoh-SAY
responses.local: De sept heures à dix heures, salle au rez-de-chaussée.

responses.phonetic: eel eh ahn-KLOO dahn voh-truh ray-zair-vah-SYOHN
responses.local: Il est inclus dans votre réservation.

phrases.intent_en: Report a problem in the room
phrases.text_local: Il y a un problème dans la chambre.
phrases.pronunciation: eel ee ah uhn prob-LEM dahn lah SHAHMBRUH
phrases.cultural_note: Point or gesture to show the issue. Common problems: 'la climatisation' (AC), 'l'eau chaude' (hot water), 'la serrure' (lock).

responses.phonetic: zhuh vooz ahn-VWAH kel-KUHN too duh SWEET
responses.local: Je vous envoie quelqu'un tout de suite.

responses.phonetic: seh kel proh-BLEM eg-zak-tuh-MAHN
responses.local: C'est quel problème exactement ?

responses.english: We can move you to another room if you'd like.
responses.phonetic: ohn puh voo shahn-ZHAY duh SHAHMBRUH see voo voo-LAY
responses.local: On peut vous changer de chambre si vous voulez.

phrases.text_local: Pouvez-vous garder nos bagages jusqu'à ce soir ?
phrases.pronunciation: poo-VAY voo gar-DAY noh bah-GAZH zhoos-kah suh SWAHR
phrases.cultural_note: Most hotels offer free luggage storage before check-in and after checkout. Ask at reception — they'll tag your bags and give you a ticket.

responses.phonetic: byahn SOOR, vwah-SEE voh-truh tee-KEH
responses.local: Bien sûr, voici votre ticket.

responses.english: You can collect your bags whenever you like.
responses.phonetic: voo poo-VAY ray-koo-pay-RAY voh bah-GAZH kahn voo voo-LAY
responses.local: Vous pouvez récupérer vos bagages quand vous voulez.

phrases.text_local: Pouvez-vous me réveiller à sept heures ?
phrases.pronunciation: poo-VAY voo muh ray-vay-YAY ah sett UR
phrases.cultural_note: Wake-up calls ('réveil téléphonique') are standard hotel services. Give a specific time — the front desk will call your room.

responses.phonetic: byahn SOOR, seh noh-TAY
responses.local: Bien sûr, c'est noté.

responses.phonetic: ah kel UR ex-ak-tuh-MAHN
responses.local: À quelle heure exactement ?

phrases.text_local: Pouvez-vous faire la chambre aujourd'hui ?
phrases.pronunciation: poo-VAY voo FAIR lah SHAHM-bruh oh-zhoor-DWEE
phrases.cultural_note: Many French hotels now skip daily cleaning to reduce environmental impact — hang the 'Please Make Up My Room' card to request it.

responses.english: Of course, we'll come in the afternoon.
responses.phonetic: byahn SOOR, noo pah-suh-ROHN dahn lah-preh-mee-DEE
responses.local: Bien sûr, nous passerons dans l'après-midi.

responses.english: Your room will be ready at 2pm.
responses.phonetic: voh-truh SHAHM-bruh suh-RAH preh ah kah-TORZ UR
responses.local: Votre chambre sera prête à quatorze heures.

phrases.text_local: Il y a beaucoup de bruit dans la chambre à côté.
phrases.pronunciation: eel ee ah boh-KOO duh BRWEE dahn lah SHAHM-bruh ah koh-TAY
phrases.cultural_note: Report noise issues to reception — they can call the neighbouring room directly. French hotels typically observe quiet hours after 10pm.

responses.phonetic: zhuh veh kohn-tak-TAY lah SHAHM-bruh ee-may-dyah-tuh-MAHN
responses.local: Je vais contacter la chambre immédiatement.

responses.english: I'm very sorry for the disturbance.
responses.phonetic: zhuh swee vreh-MAHN day-zoh-LAY poor luh day-rahnzh-MAHN
responses.local: Je suis vraiment désolé pour le dérangement.

phrases.text_local: La douche ne fonctionne pas. / Il n'y a pas d'eau chaude.
phrases.pronunciation: lah DOOSH nuh fohnk-SYOHN pah. / eel nyah pah DOH SHOHD
phrases.cultural_note: Report plumbing issues promptly — French hotel maintenance responds quickly. If the problem can't be fixed, request a room change.

responses.phonetic: zhuh veh ahn-vwah-YAY kel-KUHN too duh SWEET
responses.local: Je vais envoyer quelqu'un tout de suite.

responses.english: Would you like us to move you to another room?
responses.phonetic: voo-LAY voo kohn voo SHAHNZH duh SHAHM-bruh
responses.local: Voulez-vous qu'on vous change de chambre ?

phrases.text_local: Je voudrais faire le check-out, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: zhuh voo-DREH FAIR luh CHEK-owt, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: Standard checkout is noon in France. Hand in your key card at reception. Ask for an itemised bill ('facture détaillée') if you want to review charges.

responses.english: Of course, I'll prepare your bill.
responses.phonetic: byahn SOOR, zhuh pray-PAR voh-truh fak-TOOR
responses.local: Bien sûr, je prépare votre facture.

responses.phonetic: ah-VAY voo kohn-soh-MAY kel-kuh SHOZE oh mee-nee-BAR
responses.local: Avez-vous consommé quelque chose au minibar ?

phrases.text_local: Un billet adulte, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: uhn bee-YEH ah-DOOLT, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: Many French museums offer reduced rates ('tarif réduit') for students, seniors, and EU residents under 26.

responses.phonetic: sah feh dee-SET ZUH-roh
responses.local: Ça fait dix-sept euros.

responses.english: Do you have a student card?
responses.phonetic: vooz ah-VAY oon KART ay-too-DYAHN
responses.local: Vous avez une carte étudiant ?

responses.english: Entry is free on the first Sunday of the month.
responses.phonetic: lahn-TRAY eh grah-TWEET luh pruh-MYAY dee-MAHNSH doo MWAH
responses.local: L'entrée est gratuite le premier dimanche du mois.

phrases.text_local: Est-ce qu'on peut prendre des photos ?
phrases.pronunciation: ess kohn PUH prahndr day FOH-toh
phrases.cultural_note: Most museums allow photos without flash. Look for the crossed-out camera icon. Flash and tripods are usually banned.

responses.english: Yes, without flash and without a tripod.
responses.phonetic: wee, sahn FLASH ay sahn tray-PYAY
responses.local: Oui, sans flash et sans trépied.

responses.english: In this room, photos are not allowed.
responses.phonetic: dahn set SAL, lay FOH-toh sohn ahn-tair-DEET
responses.local: Dans cette salle, les photos sont interdites.

phrases.text_local: Vous avez un audioguide en anglais ?
phrases.pronunciation: vooz ah-VAY uhn oh-dee-oh-GEED ahn ahn-GLEH
phrases.cultural_note: Most major museums offer audio guides in English. Some now use free apps — ask 'Il y a une application ?' (Is there an app?)

responses.phonetic: wee, seh SANK ZUH-roh duh soo-play-MAHN
responses.local: Oui, c'est cinq euros de supplément.

responses.english: We also have a free app — scan this QR code.
responses.phonetic: ohn ah oh-SEE oon ah-PLEE grah-TWEET, ska-NAY suh koo-ehr KOD
responses.local: On a aussi une appli gratuite, scannez ce QR code.

phrases.text_local: Où se trouve la Joconde ?
phrases.pronunciation: oo suh TROOV lah zhoh-KOHND
phrases.cultural_note: The Mona Lisa is called 'La Joconde' in French. Replace with whatever you're looking for. Staff are usually very helpful.

responses.phonetic: SAL set-sahn-ohnz, el ree-shuh-LYUH, pruh-MYAY ay-TAZH
responses.local: Salle 711, aile Richelieu, premier étage.

responses.english: Follow the red arrows — you can't miss it.
responses.phonetic: swee-VAY lay FLESH ROOZH, voo nuh poo-VAY pah lah rah-TAY
responses.local: Suivez les flèches rouges, vous ne pouvez pas la rater.

phrases.text_local: Quelles sont vos heures d'ouverture ?
phrases.pronunciation: kel sohn voh ZUR doo-vehr-TOOR
phrases.cultural_note: Many French museums close on Mondays (national museums) or Tuesdays (city museums). Most stay open late one evening per week. Always check before visiting.

responses.phonetic: noo SOM zoo-VAIR duh dee ZUR ah dee-ZWEET UR
responses.local: Nous sommes ouverts de dix heures à dix-huit heures.

responses.english: The museum is closed on Mondays.
responses.phonetic: luh moo-ZAY eh fair-MAY luh luhn-DEE
responses.local: Le musée est fermé le lundi.

phrases.text_local: Y a-t-il des tarifs réduits ?
phrases.pronunciation: ee ah-TEEL day tah-REEF ray-DWEE
phrases.cultural_note: Many French museums offer free entry on the first Sunday of each month, and for under-26s from EU countries. Student, senior, and jobseeker discounts are common.

responses.english: Yes, entry is free for under-26s.
responses.phonetic: wee, lahn-TRAY eh gra-TWEET poor lay mwahn duh vahn-SIS AHN
responses.local: Oui, l'entrée est gratuite pour les moins de 26 ans.

responses.english: Do you have your student card?
responses.phonetic: ah-VAY voo voh-truh KART ay-too-DYAHN
responses.local: Avez-vous votre carte étudiant ?

phrases.text_local: Vous proposez des visites guidées ?
phrases.pronunciation: voo proh-poh-ZAY day vee-ZEET ghee-DAY
phrases.cultural_note: Guided tours in French museums often need advance booking. Many major museums offer tours in English — ask at the information desk or check the website.

responses.english: Yes, the next guided tour is at 2pm.
responses.phonetic: wee, lah proh-SHEN vee-ZEET ghee-DAY eh tah kah-TORZ UR
responses.local: Oui, la prochaine visite guidée est à quatorze heures.

responses.english: Guided tours are in French only.
responses.phonetic: lay vee-ZEET ghee-DAY sohn ahn frahn-SAY suhl-MAHN
responses.local: Les visites guidées sont en français seulement.

phrases.intent_en: Ask where a specific exhibit is
phrases.text_local: Où se trouve la collection…?
phrases.pronunciation: oo suh TROOV lah koh-lehk-SYOHN
phrases.cultural_note: Point to the name on a brochure or show on your phone if pronunciation is tricky. Maps ('plans') are usually available at the entrance for free.

responses.english: It's on the second floor, left wing.
responses.phonetic: seh toh duh-ZYEM ay-TAZH, el GOHSH
responses.local: C'est au deuxième étage, aile gauche.

responses.phonetic: vwah-SEE luh PLAHN doo moo-ZAY
responses.local: Voici le plan du musée.

phrases.text_local: Le musée est accessible aux personnes en fauteuil roulant ?
phrases.pronunciation: luh moo-ZAY eh tak-seh-SEE-bluh oh pair-SON ahn foh-TUY roo-LAHN
phrases.cultural_note: French museums are legally required to provide accessibility. Lifts, ramps, and tactile trails are standard in major institutions. Wheelchairs are often available to borrow free of charge.

responses.english: Yes, there's a lift and access ramps.
responses.phonetic: wee, eel ee ah uhn ah-sahn-SUR ay day RAHMP dak-SEH
responses.local: Oui, il y a un ascenseur et des rampes d'accès.

responses.english: You can borrow a wheelchair free of charge.
responses.phonetic: voo poo-VAY ahn-proon-TAY uhn foh-TUY roo-LAHN grah-TWEET-mahn
responses.local: Vous pouvez emprunter un fauteuil roulant gratuitement.

phrases.text_local: Excusez-moi, où est le rayon eau ?
phrases.pronunciation: ex-koo-ZAY mwah, oo eh luh ray-OHN OH
phrases.cultural_note: 'Rayon' means aisle/section. Common ones: 'fruits et légumes' (produce), 'boulangerie' (bread), 'boissons' (drinks).

responses.english: It's aisle four, at the back on the right.
responses.phonetic: seh ah-LAY KATR, oh FOHN ah DRWAHT
responses.local: C'est allée quatre, au fond à droite.

responses.phonetic: zhuh nuh trah-VY pah ee-SEE, day-zoh-LAY
responses.local: Je ne travaille pas ici, désolé.

phrases.text_local: Vous avez des sacs ?
phrases.pronunciation: vooz ah-VAY day SAK
phrases.cultural_note: Plastic bags are banned in France. Bring your own or buy a reusable bag at the register (usually €0.10–€1).

responses.english: Yes, reusable bags for fifty cents.
responses.phonetic: wee, day SAK ray-oo-tee-lee-ZAHBL ah sahn-KAHNT sahn-TEEM
responses.local: Oui, des sacs réutilisables à cinquante centimes.

responses.phonetic: nohn, lay SAK plah-STEEK sohn ahn-tair-DEE
responses.local: Non, les sacs plastique sont interdits.

phrases.intent_en: Ask if you can pay by card
phrases.text_local: Je peux payer par carte ?
phrases.pronunciation: zhuh PUH pay-YAY par KART
phrases.cultural_note: Card is widely accepted. Contactless ('sans contact') works for under €50. Some small shops have a minimum amount.

responses.english: Yes, contactless up to fifty euros.
responses.phonetic: wee, sahn kohn-TAKT zhoos-KAH sahn-KAHNT ZUH-roh
responses.local: Oui, sans contact jusqu'à cinquante euros.

responses.english: Sorry, card only from ten euros.
responses.phonetic: day-zoh-LAY, KART suhl-MAHN ah par-TEER duh DEE ZUH-roh
responses.local: Désolé, carte seulement à partir de dix euros.

phrases.text_local: Je regarde, merci.
phrases.pronunciation: zhuh ruh-GARD, mair-SEE
phrases.cultural_note: Useful if a shop assistant approaches you. It's polite but clearly signals you don't need help right now.

responses.english: No problem — call me if you need anything.
responses.phonetic: pah duh soo-SEE, ah-play-YAY mwah see vooz ah-VAY buh-ZWAHN
responses.local: Pas de souci, appelez-moi si vous avez besoin.

responses.english: OK, I'm here if you have any questions.
responses.phonetic: dah-KOR, zhuh swee LAH see vooz ah-VAY oon kes-TYOHN
responses.local: D'accord, je suis là si vous avez une question.

phrases.text_local: Où est la caisse, s'il vous plaît ?
phrases.pronunciation: oo eh lah KES, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: In French supermarkets, self-checkouts ('caisses automatiques') are increasingly common. Staffed tills are often labelled by number of items — express lanes ('caisse rapide') are usually for 10 items or fewer.

responses.english: The checkout is at the back on the right.
responses.phonetic: lah KES eh toh FOHN ah DRWAHT
responses.local: La caisse est au fond à droite.

responses.english: The self-checkouts are right in front of you.
responses.phonetic: lay KES oh-toh-mah-TEEK sohn duh-VAHN voo
responses.local: Les caisses automatiques sont devant vous.

phrases.intent_en: Say you don't need a bag
phrases.text_local: Non merci, je n'ai pas besoin de sac.
phrases.pronunciation: nohn mehr-SEE, zhuh nay pah buh-ZWAHN duh SAHK
phrases.cultural_note: Single-use plastic bags are banned in French supermarkets. Reusable bags ('sacs réutilisables') are sold at the till. If you have your own bag, saying this saves you the charge.

responses.phonetic: dah-KOR, mehr-SEE
responses.local: D'accord, merci.

responses.english: Do you have a bag with you?
responses.phonetic: voo zah-VAY uhn SAHK ah-VEK voo
responses.local: Vous avez un sac avec vous ?

phrases.text_local: Quel est le prix de ceci, s'il vous plaît ?
phrases.pronunciation: kel eh luh PREE duh suh-SEE, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: Useful when a price tag is missing or the shelf price looks wrong. Staff ('employés') can scan the barcode with a handheld scanner. At the till, you can also query a price before paying.

responses.phonetic: seh DUH ZUH-roh sank-AHNT
responses.local: C'est deux euros cinquante.

responses.phonetic: zhuh veh luh ska-NAY poor voo
responses.local: Je vais le scanner pour vous.

phrases.intent_en: Ask about returning or exchanging something
phrases.text_local: Je voudrais faire un retour ou un échange, s'il vous plaît.
phrases.pronunciation: zhuh voo-DREH fair uhn ruh-TOOR oo uhn ay-SHAHNGZH, seel voo PLEH
phrases.cultural_note: In French supermarkets, returns are generally accepted with a receipt ('ticket de caisse') within a reasonable timeframe. Perishable or opened items cannot usually be returned. Go to the customer service desk ('service clients').

responses.phonetic: voo zah-VAY VOH-truh tee-KAY duh KES
responses.local: Vous avez votre ticket de caisse ?

responses.english: Customer service is at the back of the shop.
responses.phonetic: luh sehr-VEES klee-AHN eh toh FOHN doo mah-gah-ZAN
responses.local: Le service clients est au fond du magasin.

phrases.intent_en: Ask for help with the self-checkout machine
phrases.text_local: Je ne comprends pas la caisse automatique. Pouvez-vous m'aider ?
phrases.pronunciation: zhuh nuh kohm-PRAHN pah lah KES oh-toh-mah-TEEK. poo-VAY voo may-DAY
phrases.cultural_note: Self-checkout machines in France have a member of staff ('assistant de caisse') overseeing several tills. They expect these questions — just wave or call out and they'll come straight over.

responses.english: Of course, I'll be right there.
responses.phonetic: byahn SOOR, zhuh VYAHN too duh SWEET
responses.local: Bien sûr, je viens tout de suite.

responses.phonetic: skah-NAY dah-BOR luh kohd-BAR
responses.local: Scannez d'abord le code-barres.

phrases.text_local: Je ne me sens pas bien.
phrases.pronunciation: zhuh nuh muh SAHN pah BYEHN
phrases.cultural_note: French pharmacists are trained to advise on minor ailments. They can often recommend treatment without a prescription.

responses.phonetic: kess kee nuh vah PAH
responses.local: Qu'est-ce qui ne va pas ?

responses.english: How long have you been feeling this way?
responses.phonetic: duh-PWEE kohm-BYEHN duh TAHN
responses.local: Depuis combien de temps ?

responses.phonetic: vooz ah-VAY duh lah fee-EVRUH
responses.local: Vous avez de la fièvre ?

phrases.text_local: Vous avez quelque chose contre les maux de tête ?
phrases.pronunciation: vooz ah-VAY kel-kuh SHOZE kohntr lay MOH duh TET
phrases.cultural_note: Common brands: Doliprane (paracetamol), Nurofen (ibuprofen). Pharmacists will ask about allergies.

responses.phonetic: vooz ET ah-lair-ZHEEK ah kel-kuh SHOZE
responses.local: Vous êtes allergique à quelque chose ?

responses.phonetic: zhuh voo kohn-SAY luh doh-lee-PRAHN
responses.local: Je vous conseille le Doliprane.

responses.phonetic: pruh-NAY ahn uhn toot lay SEE ZUHr
responses.local: Prenez-en un toutes les six heures.

phrases.text_local: Je cherche de la crème solaire.
phrases.pronunciation: zhuh SHERSH duh lah KREM soh-LAIR
phrases.cultural_note: French pharmacies stock high-quality sunscreens (La Roche-Posay, Avène). They're often cheaper there than in shops.

responses.english: Which SPF do you want — 30, 50, or 100?
responses.phonetic: kel ahn-DEES voo voo-LAY — TRAHNT, sahn-KAHNT oo SAHN
responses.local: Quel indice vous voulez — trente, cinquante ou cent ?

responses.english: Here you go — the La Roche-Posay is very good.
responses.phonetic: vwah-LAH, lah lah rosh-poh-ZAY eh treh BYEHN
responses.local: Voilà, la La Roche-Posay est très bien.

phrases.text_local: J'ai mal au ventre. Vous avez quelque chose ?
phrases.pronunciation: zhay mal oh VAHNTRUH. vooz ah-VAY kel-kuh SHOZE
phrases.cultural_note: The pharmacist will ask follow-up questions. Point to where it hurts. Smecta and Spasfon are common French remedies.

responses.english: Is it more cramping or nausea?
responses.phonetic: seh ploo-TOH day KRAMP oo duh lah noh-ZAY
responses.local: C'est plutôt des crampes ou de la nausée ?

responses.english: I'll give you some Smecta — it's very effective.
responses.phonetic: zhuh voo DON doo SMEK-tah, seh treh ef-ee-KAS
responses.local: Je vous donne du Smecta, c'est très efficace.

responses.phonetic: vooz ah-VAY mahn-ZHAY kel-kuh SHOZE duh par-tee-koo-LYAY
responses.local: Vous avez mangé quelque chose de particulier ?

phrases.text_local: J'ai un coup de soleil. Qu'est-ce que vous conseillez ?
phrases.pronunciation: zhay uhn KOO duh soh-LAY. kess kuh voo kohn-say-YAY
phrases.cultural_note: Biafine is the go-to French sunburn cream — pharmacists will likely recommend it. Available without prescription.

responses.english: I recommend Biafine — apply it often.
responses.phonetic: zhuh voo ruh-koh-MAHND lah byah-FEEN, ah-plee-KAY ahn soo-VAHN
responses.local: Je vous recommande la Biafine, appliquez-en souvent.

responses.english: Avoid the sun and drink plenty of water.
responses.phonetic: ay-vee-TAY luh soh-LAY ay boo-VAY boh-KOO DOH
responses.local: Évitez le soleil et buvez beaucoup d'eau.

phrases.intent_en: Ask for something for a cold or flu
phrases.text_local: J'ai un rhume / une grippe. Qu'est-ce que vous avez ?
phrases.pronunciation: zhay uhn ROOM / oon GREEP. kess kuh vooz ah-VAY
phrases.cultural_note: Common French cold remedies: Fervex, Actifed, Humex. Pharmacists will ask about specific symptoms — fever, cough, runny nose — before recommending.

responses.english: I recommend this syrup and these tablets.
responses.phonetic: zhuh voo ruh-koh-MAHND suh see-ROH ay seh kohm-pree-MAY
